ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Emily Jones

value.displayName: if amoxicillin failure or recent antibiotic use
for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration

act.text: HPI (Parent Narrative): Emily woke up yesterday morning with a fever—I took her temperature and it was 102.5. She's been complaining that her head hurts and her throat is sore, and she just doesn't want to do anything. I gave her some ibuprofen and she felt a little better for a few hours, but the fever came back this afternoon. She won't eat much, just wants to drink water and lie on the couch. I'm mostly worried it might be strep or something that needs antibiotics.

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 78) and unremarkable systemic exam (normal HEENT, clear lungs bilaterally) suggests benign viral etiology; further workup warranted if fever persists or additional symptoms develop.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"She got bitten by something yesterday afternoon while playing outside, and we didn't think much of it at first. But this morning the bite on her leg was really swollen and red, and now there are a few more bites on her arms that are doing the same thing. We put some calamine lotion on it and gave her some Benadryl, but it just keeps getting bigger and itchier. We're worried it might be infected or that she's having some kind of allergic reaction."

Insect Bite Reaction: Clinical presentation consistent with insect bite reaction: localized inflammatory response to arthropod bite with normal vital signs (HR 88) and unremarkable systemic exam (clear lungs, normal HEENT), ruling out systemic involvement or anaphylaxis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 10 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been sneezy all day. This morning it got worse and now she's complaining that her nose feels stuffy. We gave her some children's cold medicine and have been pushing fluids, but she's still pretty congested. We're mainly worried because she has soccer tryouts this weekend and we want to make sure it's not something serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral inflammation of upper airway, while clear lungs and normal vital signs (HR 85) exclude lower respiratory involvement and systemic toxicity.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 600mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 400mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She got bitten by something yesterday afternoon while playing outside, and we didn't think much of it at first. But this morning the area around the bite got really red and puffy, and now there are a few more bites that are doing the same thing. We put some hydrocortisone cream on it and gave her some Benadryl, but it just keeps getting bigger and itchier. We're worried it might be infected or that she's having some kind of allergic reaction we don't know about."

Insect Bite Reaction: Clinical presentation consistent w/ insect bite reaction: localized inflammatory response to arthropod bite w/ normal vital signs (HR 94) and unremarkable systemic exam (HEENT nl, lungs CTA bilaterally) excluding systemic involvement or anaphylaxis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 9 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 8 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been sneezy all day. This morning she complained that her throat hurt a little bit, but she's been eating okay and playing normally. We gave her some Tylenol yesterday and she's been drinking plenty of fluids. We're mostly just concerned it doesn't turn into something worse, like an ear infection or bronchitis like she had last winter."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lungs on auscultation support upper respiratory involvement without lower respiratory tract infection or bacterial pharyngitis.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 460mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 310mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up yesterday morning complaining that her ear hurt, and by afternoon her temperature was up to 102. She's been really fussy and won't eat much because it hurts when she swallows. We gave her ibuprofen and it helped a little bit, but the fever keeps coming back and she's been tugging at her ear. I'm worried it's a bad ear infection and want to make sure she gets better before she misses more school."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media: fever, erythematous and bulging TM with loss of light reflex, decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, and middle ear effusion all indicate acute inflammation and fluid accumulation in the middle ear space.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 2750mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 2750mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 310mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been sneezy all day. This morning she complained that her throat felt scratchy and she's been coughing a little bit, mostly at night. We gave her some children's cold medicine and have been pushing fluids, but she's not really interested in eating much. She seems okay otherwise—still playing—but we wanted to get her checked out since she has school next week and we wanted to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: History and exam findings consistent w/ viral URI: clear rhinorrhea and mild erythema of nasal mucosa and oropharynx without exudate or systemic toxicity support upper respiratory viral etiology. Absence of focal findings on lung exam and normal vital signs (HR 91) exclude lower respiratory involvement and serious bacterial infection.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 460mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 310mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She's been complaining that her ear hurts since yesterday afternoon, right after swim team practice. This morning it was worse and she said it felt like water was stuck in there. We tried drying it out with a hair dryer on low and gave her some ibuprofen, but it's still bothering her. I'm mainly worried it's an infection and want to make sure it gets treated before it gets worse."

Swimmer's Ear: Clinical presentation consistent w/ otitis externa (swimmer's ear): localized ear canal inflammation from water exposure. Exam findings unremarkable except for ear pathology, ruling out systemic infection or trauma; mild tachycardia (HR 102) likely secondary to discomfort/fever rather than systemic compromise.
- [medication] Amoxicillin 90mg/kg/day divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen for pain management - [follow-up] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours
Well-child examination - 7 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever—it was 101.5 when I checked. She's been complaining that her throat hurts and she won't eat much, just wants to drink juice. I gave her ibuprofen yesterday and it brought the fever down for a few hours, but it came back up last night to 102. She's been pretty fussy and clingy, which isn't like her. I'm mostly worried because the fever won't stay down and I want to make sure it's not strep or something serious."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 86) and unremarkable exam findings (normocephalic head, clear lungs bilaterally, normal HEENT) supports diagnosis of fever; further workup needed to identify underlying etiology as vitals and exam do not reveal focal infection source.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 6 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with some red spots on her chest and belly. They've been spreading throughout the day, and now they're on her arms and legs too. We gave her some children's Benadryl last night thinking it might be an allergic reaction, but it doesn't seem to have helped. She's been her normal self otherwise—eating and playing fine—but I'm worried because I've never seen a rash like this before and it just keeps getting worse."

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash with normal vital signs (HR 88) and unremarkable systemic exam (clear lungs, normal HEENT) supports benign dermatologic process without evidence of systemic involvement or acute illness.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and was a little fussy, but we didn't think much of it. By last night her nose got really stuffy and she developed a fever—we took her temperature this morning and it was over 101. She's been complaining that her nose is uncomfortable and won't eat much, just wants to drink juice. We gave her some children's cold medicine and a humidifier last night, but the fever hasn't really come down, so we wanted to get her checked out to make sure it's not something more serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, and mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate support upper respiratory involvement; clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 340mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 230mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 8 o'clock. At first I thought it was just once, but then she kept doing it every couple hours even when she wasn't eating anything. She's had some diarrhea too and just seems really tired and cranky. I've been giving her small sips of water and crackers like I normally do, but she won't keep anything down. I'm mostly worried because she hasn't had a wet diaper since this morning and I want to make sure she doesn't get dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: mild tachycardia (HR 104) likely 2° to fluid loss/dehydration, while normal HEENT & clear lungs exclude alternate diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia) & suggest uncomplicated GI etiology.

- [medication] Ondansetron 3mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"She started throwing up yesterday afternoon after lunch, and then had diarrhea a few times last night. This morning she seemed a little better but then got sick again, and now she's just really tired and won't eat anything. We've been giving her small sips of water and some crackers, but she's not keeping much down. We're mainly worried because she's acting pretty listless and we want to make sure she's not getting dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 116) suggests mild dehydration from fluid losses, while normal HEENT & lungs rule out complications or alternative diagnoses (e.g., pneumonia, meningitis).

- [medication] Ondansetron 3mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 5 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"Emily woke up yesterday morning saying her ear hurt, and she's been really fussy ever since. She's been running a fever on and off, won't eat much, and keeps pulling at her right ear. We gave her some children's pain reliever and a warm compress, but she's still uncomfortable and slept poorly last night. I'm worried it's getting worse and want to make sure it's not something serious."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings consistent with AOM: TM erythema, bulging, and loss of light reflex with decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy indicate acute inflammation and middle ear effusion; fever and tachycardia support acute infection. Absence of respiratory findings excludes concurrent URI as primary diagnosis.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1800mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1800mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 200mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

Emily started with a runny nose and slight cough three days ago. At first it was just clear mucus, but yesterday she became fussier than usual and didn't want to eat much. We've been giving her fluids and tried some saline drops in her nose, which helped a little. We're mainly worried because she's not acting like herself and we want to make sure it's not something more serious.

Upper Respiratory Infection: History and exam findings consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lung fields support upper respiratory involvement without lower respiratory tract disease or bacterial superinfection. Mild tachycardia (HR 105) is expected compensatory response in acute viral illness.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 300mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 200mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She got a scrape on her knee when she fell off the playground equipment about 5 days ago, and we cleaned it up with soap and water. Starting yesterday, we noticed some yellow crusty stuff forming around the scrape, and now it's spreading to a couple other spots on her legs where she's been scratching. We put some antibiotic ointment on it last night, but it doesn't seem to be getting any better. We're worried it's getting infected and don't want it to spread to her face or other kids at school."

Impetigo: Clinical presentation consistent w/ impetigo: localized pyogenic skin infection in preschool-age child. Normal vitals (HR 109 age-appropriate) & clear lungs r/o systemic involvement; HEENT exam confirms lesions localized to skin without complications.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 4 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started throwing up yesterday afternoon, maybe around 2 o'clock, and has had diarrhea since then—I'd say about 6 or 7 times. She's been running a fever too, we checked it this morning and it was over 101. She won't eat anything, just wants to drink, but even the water seems to come right back up sometimes. We've been giving her small sips and tried some crackers yesterday but that didn't help. I'm mostly worried she's getting dehydrated because her lips look dry and she's not her normal self—just lying around and fussy." --- Key elements included: - Onset: "yesterday afternoon" - Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea (frequency noted), fever - Progression: escalating symptoms - Intake attempts: water, crackers - Parental concern: dehydration (correlates with clinical finding of dry mucous membranes) - Natural parental language and observation style

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: fever, tachycardia (HR 102), and mild dehydration (dry mucous membranes) support acute infectious gastroenteritis; normal pulmonary and neurologic exams exclude alternative diagnoses.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"She started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 8 o'clock, and it's happened about five or six times since then. This morning she had diarrhea too—it's been loose and watery. She's not interested in eating anything, though I've been trying to give her sips of water and some juice like my mom suggested. I'm mostly worried because she seems really tired and fussy, and I want to make sure she doesn't get dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent with viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 114) and slightly dry mucous membranes indicate mild dehydration from GI losses, while normal lung/HEENT exams exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, meningitis). Normocephalic, atraumatic head exam and clear lungs support primary GI etiology without systemic complications.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 3 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with her left eye all red and crusty. There's a yellowish discharge coming out of it, and she keeps rubbing at it. We tried wiping it with a warm washcloth this morning, but it's still pretty goopy and her eye looks really irritated. We're worried it might be an infection and want to make sure she doesn't spread it to her sister."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings of conjunctival injection with purulent discharge and eyelid edema are consistent with bacterial conjunctivitis. Normal corneal exam and visual acuity exclude keratitis and more serious anterior segment pathology.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia

HPI (Parent Narrative): Emily woke up fussy yesterday morning and has been tugging at her right ear on and off. She's had a low fever since last night—we took her temperature this morning and it was 100.6. She's not eating much and seems uncomfortable, especially when she lies down. We gave her some children's ibuprofen yesterday which helped a little, but we're worried it might be an ear infection since she keeps grabbing at her ear.

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings consistent with AOM: fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, and classic otoscopic findings (erythematous, bulging TM with absent light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy) indicate acute middle ear inflammation with effusion. Clear lungs exclude concurrent lower respiratory infection.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1400mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1400mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 150mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and seemed fine, but then last night she developed this really barky cough—it sounds like a seal—and her voice got all raspy. She's had a fever since this morning, around 101. We gave her some cool mist from the bathroom and some water, but the cough keeps coming back, especially when she cries or gets upset. We're worried because she's never sounded like this before and we're not sure if she's getting worse or better."

Croup: Clinical presentation consistent with croup: hoarse voice/cry + barky cough with fever and tachypnea (RR 28) in 2 yo, characteristic of viral laryngotracheobronchitis. Clear lung fields exclude lower airway involvement/pneumonia, supporting upper airway etiology.

- [medication] Dexamethasone 9mg x1 dose x 1 days - [education] Single dose of dexamethasone is typically curative - [education] Cool mist or night air may provide temporary relief - [education] Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY for stridor at rest, drooling, or respiratory distress

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever - we took her temperature and it was 102.5. She's been really fussy and clingy all day, won't eat much, just wants her bottle. We gave her some Tylenol this morning and it came down a bit, but it keeps going back up. We're worried because she's never had a fever this high before and we want to make sure it's not something serious."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with tachycardia (HR 118) and otherwise benign exam (normal HEENT, clear lungs bilaterally) is consistent with acute febrile illness, likely viral etiology. Absence of focal findings on exam supports systemic rather than localized infection at this time.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy all day. Last night she had a fever—we took her temperature and it was 101.5, so we gave her some Tylenol and she seemed a little better. Today she's not eating much, just picking at her food, and she keeps tugging at her ears. We're worried it might be an ear infection since her older brother had one last month."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 125) appropriate for age/fever, normal HEENT exam r/o bacterial sinusitis/otitis, and clear lungs b/l r/o lower respiratory tract involvement.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Tympanostomy Tubes

Mother reports that Emily woke up yesterday morning with her right eye "all red and crusty." She says the eye has been draining yellow stuff throughout the day, and Emily keeps rubbing it and complaining it hurts. This morning the left eye started looking the same way. Mom tried wiping it clean with a warm washcloth and has been keeping her home, but she's worried it might be an infection and wants to make sure Emily doesn't need antibiotics.

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings consistent with conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection with discharge noted on HEENT exam, with intact cornea and normal visual acuity excluding keratitis or more serious pathology. Tachycardia and tachypnea likely represent systemic response to infection; clear lung fields rule out concurrent respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. She's been coughing a lot and won't eat much—just drinking some juice and water. We gave her children's Tylenol yesterday which brought the fever down a little, but it came back up this morning and now she's at 103. We're worried because she's so clingy and just wants to be held, which isn't like her."

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent w/ influenza: viral URI sx w/ fever, tachycardia (HR 104), tachypnea (RR 29) suggesting systemic viral infection; lungs clear bilaterally excludes pneumonia at this time.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She had a little scrape on her knee from falling down a few days ago, but then yesterday we noticed some yellowish crusting started showing up on it. Today it's gotten bigger and spread to her other knee, and there's some oozing. We've been cleaning it with soap and water and putting Neosporin on it, but it just keeps getting worse. We're worried it's infected and want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Impetigo: Clinical presentation consistent w/ impetigo: localized pustular/crusted lesions on exposed skin surfaces typical of this superficial bacterial infection in toddler-age child. Vital signs stable (HR 120 age-appropriate) w/ no systemic toxicity on exam, supporting uncomplicated impetigo.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up this morning with a red rash all over her body—it started on her chest and spread everywhere by this afternoon. She's been really fussy and pulling at her ears, and she doesn't want to eat her usual breakfast. We gave her some baby Tylenol about an hour ago but the rash is still there. We're worried it might be something serious since she's never had a rash like this before."

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash in 11 mo F with vital sign abnormalities (HR 132, RR 34) and otherwise benign exam (normocephalic, clear lungs) supports infectious etiology. Tachycardia and tachypnea suggest systemic response to infection, consistent w/ common viral exanthems of infancy.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever

"She's been running a fever since yesterday morning—we took her temperature and it was around 101. She's been really fussy and won't take her bottles like normal, just picks at them. This morning she had some loose stools, maybe two or three times. We gave her some infant acetaminophen yesterday which helped a little bit, but she's still not acting like herself. We're mostly worried because she's not eating well and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 127) and tachypnea (RR 29) suggest systemic viral infection with compensatory response, while clear lungs and benign HEENT exam exclude acute bacterial complications (otitis media, pneumonia).
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 8 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Emily started having a runny nose about 3 days ago with clear drainage. Her mom noticed she's been a bit fussier than usual and isn't eating as much as she normally does. They tried saline nose drops yesterday to help clear her nose, but mom is concerned because Emily seems uncomfortable and has been sneezing a lot. Mom denies fever, cough, or any vomiting.

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa and mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate indicate viral upper respiratory involvement. Tachycardia (HR 139) and tachypnea (RR 35) are expected compensatory responses to systemic viral infection in this age group, with clear lung fields excluding lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 120mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 80mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"Emily started getting a runny nose yesterday morning, and today she's been pretty fussy and has a fever. She's had clear stuff coming out of her nose all day, and she's not eating as much as usual—just picking at her bottles. We gave her some infant acetaminophen this morning when we took her temperature and it was 100.5, and that seemed to help a little bit, but we're worried because she's so young. She's still having wet diapers and hasn't had any cough or trouble breathing, but we wanted to get her checked out since she's only 4 months old."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation; fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea are expected systemic responses in infants with viral URI, while clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 100mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She started getting a runny nose and seemed a little fussy yesterday morning. Today she's been running a fever—we took her temperature and it was over 101. She's not eating as much as usual and has been pretty sleepy, which worries us. We've been giving her cool baths and keeping her hydrated, but we wanted to make sure it's nothing serious since she's still so young."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever, rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, and mild oropharyngeal erythema in 4 mo F with clear lungs and normal respiratory effort, suggesting uncomplicated viral URI without lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 100mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 4 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Healthy newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
